did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel deep learning model called Adaptive Efficient Core Quaternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network (AdECQ-FH-BSNet) for skin cancer detection and classification. The method incorporates deep learning components such as Efficient Cascade Convolution (ECC) for segmentation, Core Components of Quaternions (CCQ) for feature extraction, and Binary Spiking Network (BSNet) for classification, optimized by the Fire Hawk Optimizer (FHO). The study was conducted on the HAM10000 dermatological dataset to diagnose skin cancers like melanoma and actinic keratosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel deep learning model called AdECQ-FH-BSNet for skin cancer detection, detailing its specific methodology (Adaptive Iteration Guide Filter, Efficient Cascade Convolution, Core Components of Quaternions, Binary Spiking Network, and Fire Hawk Optimizer). It reports original experimental results, including performance metrics (99.8% recall, 99.9% accuracy) on the HAM10000 dataset, and compares them with existing methods. Although it includes a literature survey, the primary contribution is the proposal and validation of a new technical approach, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Adaptive Efficient Core Quanternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network using Skin Cancer Detection on Big Data Healthcare
journal: Proceedings of the 6th International Conference on Inventive Research in Computing Applications (ICIRCA-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes the Adaptive Efficient Core Quaternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network (AdECQ-FH-BSNet) for skin cancer detection and classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The method employs an Adaptive Iteration Guide Filter for noise reduction, Efficient Cascade Convolution for segmentation, Core Components of Quaternions for feature extraction, and a Binary Spiking Network for classification, all optimized by the Fire Hawk Optimizer. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains over 10,000 dermatoscopic images of benign and malignant lesions. The proposed approach achieved superior performance compared to existing methods, reaching 99.9% accuracy and 99.8% recall. These results demonstrate the model's effectiveness in accurately distinguishing between skin cancer types, such as melanoma and actinic keratosis, for early diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the most deadly type of skin cancer; the HAM10000 dataset includes melanoma classes, and the proposed model (AdECQ-FH-BSNet) was evaluated on it. Table 2 lists 'MEL' (Melanoma) as a class with 99.9% accuracy for the proposed technique.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion classes (e.g., Melanoma, Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of a Binary Spiking Network (BSNet) for 'classification' of skin cancer types. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset, which contains seven classes of benign and malignant lesions. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of annotated dermoscopic skin lesion images from various individuals, including metadata such as age, sex, and lesion kind. It contains benign and malignant lesions.

use.training: Used to train the AdECQ-FH-BSNet model.
use.validation: Used to validate the model (loss and accuracy tracked over 100 epochs).
use.testing: Used to evaluate performance metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score) for classes like Actinic Keratosis and Melanoma.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against other methods (DL-RNet, Ef-INet, TNet, DL-TFK).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score, and Mean Square Error (MSE).

HAM10000.notes: The proposed AdECQ-FH-BSNet outperforms existing methods (DL-RNet, Ef-INet, TNet, DL-TFK) on the HAM10000 dataset. The model achieved 99.9% accuracy and 99.8% recall. The study also references comparative results from prior literature: RegNetY-320 (91% accuracy, 88.1% F1-score), Mobile Net (90% top-1 accuracy), and Efficient Net B4 (87.91% top-1 accuracy, 87% F1-score). Table 2 in the full paper reportedly lists per-class accuracy for Melanoma (99.9%) and Actinic Keratosis (99.4%). The model was trained for 100 epochs with loss flattening indicating convergence.

architecture_used.main_model: Adaptive Efficient Core Quaternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network (AdECQ-FH-BSNet)

components: Adaptive Iteration Guide Filter (AdIF) for image pre-processing and noise reduction
Efficient Cascade Convolution (ECC) with Graph Attention Convolutional Layer for segmentation
Core Components of Quaternions (CCQ) for feature extraction using Quaternion Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs)
Binary Spiking Network (BSNet) for classification using intensity-to-latency conversion
Fire Hawk Optimizer (FHO) for parameter optimization
architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Core Components of Quaternions (CCQ) utilizing skew-field algebraic structure and Quaternion Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs)
architecture_details.classification_method: Binary Spiking Network (BSNet) with single-spike temporal coding (intensity-to-latency conversion)

training_details.convergence_behavior: Loss flattens off after early epochs, indicating convergence

classification_or_output_head.output: Classification of skin cancer types (malignant vs non-cancerous/benign)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Adaptive Efficient Core Quaternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network (AdECQ-FH-BSNet)
preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Iteration Guide Filter (AdIF) for noise reduction and artifact removal while preserving edge details

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as part of the pre-processing pipeline for the HAM10000 dataset in the context of Efficient Nets (Ali et al.) and general preprocessing steps, but no specific techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping) or probabilities are reported for the proposed AdECQ-FH-BSNet model.
Used in the referenced study by Alam et al. [6] to balance the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset classes; specific augmentation methods are not detailed in the provided context.

conclusions: The proposed Adaptive Efficient Core Quaternions Fire Hawk Binary Spiking Network (AdECQ-FH-BSNet) significantly advances medical image processing for skin cancer diagnosis.

The model achieves superior performance on the HAM10000 dataset compared to existing methods (DL-RNet, Ef-INet, TNet, DL-TFK), with 99.4% accuracy for Actinic Keratosis and 99.7% accuracy for Melanoma.

The AdECQ-FH-BSNet demonstrates high reliability with precision, recall, and sensitivity values approaching 99% for malignant cases and specificity values of 99.4% for malignant and 98.7% for benign cases.
The integration of the Fire Hawk Optimizer (FHO) effectively prevents local optima and optimizes network parameters, leading to faster convergence and improved accuracy in large healthcare datasets.
The method is suitable for big data healthcare applications, enabling early identification and diagnosis of skin cancer to enhance patient outcomes through prompt interventions.

The study acknowledges that while the proposed method is effective, issues such as data privacy, standardization, and model interpretability remain challenges in the broader field of big data healthcare.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases; no external validation on independent dermatological datasets was performed.

Performance metrics for comparative models (DL-RNet, Ef-INet, TNet, DL-TFK) are taken from other cited papers rather than re-evaluated on the same experimental setup, limiting the validity of direct performance comparisons.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported performance gains.

The model was implemented and tested in a non-clinical software environment (Python 3.7, Windows 10), raising concerns about deployment feasibility in standard clinical hardware or cloud infrastructure.
The authors acknowledge but do not address critical clinical deployment barriers such as model interpretability, data privacy compliance, and standardization of input imaging modalities.

The proposed method uses a complex hybrid architecture (Quaternions, Spiking Networks, Meta-heuristic Optimization) which may introduce high computational overhead and complexity compared to simpler CNN baselines, potentially hindering real-time clinical adoption.
